The witnesses note Jacob cursing the anger of Simeon and Levi and foretelling their scattering. Gill notes their anger was sinful in its nature, criminal and detestable, strong, fierce, and furious in its operation, and so justly cursed; not the persons, but the sin. The witnesses note Jacob cursing their fierce anger and cruel wrath, and declaring he would divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel; the sentence on their sin and its strange fulfilment in the scattering of the two tribes — that Jacob curses the sin and foretells the punishment: "Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel: I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel"; the witnesses note the careful aim of the curse, "Cursed be their anger... and their wrath" — that Jacob curses not the persons of his sons but their "anger" and "wrath," the sinful passion itself, "fierce" and "cruel," justly accursed; the witnesses note the sentence, "I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel" — that the two tribes should be scattered and not have a settled, undivided inheritance of their own; the witnesses note the remarkable fulfilment the commentators trace — that Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled, while Levi received no land at all but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel (yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood); the witnesses note the truth that God can make even a curse the means of mercy, as in Levi; so that Jacob curses their wrath and foretells their scattering; I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel.
